Front: What does it mean that Kubernetes 'eliminates the need for orchestration'?
Back: Orchestration implies executing a preordained workflow (do A, then B, then C). Kubernetes instead runs independent control loops that continuously compare actual state to a declared desired state and act to close the gap, forever. It converges to desired state rather than following scripted steps; this is also what gives self-healing.

Front: Why is Kubernetes NOT a Platform as a Service (PaaS)?
Back: Because it operates at the container level, not the application level, and omits opinionated app-level services: it does not build your app from source, ships no built-in database/message-queue/middleware, does not dictate language/framework, and leaves logging/monitoring pluggable. It gives flexible building blocks (you can build PaaS-like things on top); a PaaS gives a managed app platform with choices made for you.

Front: Control plane vs worker nodes: roles, and where do pods run?
Back: Control plane = the brain: global decisions (scheduling), watches and responds to cluster events, serves the API, stores truth in etcd. Worker nodes = the muscle: the kubelet keeps assigned pods' containers running. Pods physically run on worker nodes. A node is itself a machine (VM/physical); a cluster spans many, with control plane usually on separate machines.

Front: You declare 3 replicas; a node crashes and kills one. What restores it, and by what mechanism?
Back: A controller's reconciliation loop. The ReplicaSet controller (managed by a Deployment) in the kube-controller-manager compares actual (2) vs desired (3): the node controller marks the lost pods gone, the ReplicaSet controller creates a replacement, the kube-scheduler assigns it to a healthy node, and that node's kubelet runs it. Self-healing = the desired-state loop doing its normal job.

Front: What is the cloud-controller-manager and why does it matter for GCP?
Back: An optional control-plane component that integrates Kubernetes with a cloud provider's APIs (load balancers, nodes, routes). On GKE (Google's managed Kubernetes) it is the seam where Kubernetes meets GCP, so it is directly relevant to the GCP ML Engineer path.

Front: How does 3Sum reduce to Two Sum II, and what is its time complexity?
Back: Sort the array, then for each fixed FIRST element nums[i], run a Two Sum II two-pointer on the elements to its right looking for a pair summing to -nums[i]; collect all unique triplets. Time O(n^2): sort O(n log n) is dominated by n outer iterations each running an O(n) two-pointer.

Front: When is the two-pointer technique valid? What is the signal to reach for it?
Back: When each pointer move eliminates part of the search space without backtracking. Two flavors: (1) sorted/monotonic data, so moving a pointer changes a value predictably (Two Sum II, 3Sum); (2) comparing from both ends of a symmetric structure (Valid Palindrome). Sortedness is the most common tell, but symmetry counts too.

Both give correct unique triplets and O(n^2). Converting results to a set of tuples is simpler but still generates duplicates and re-scans for repeated fixed elements (e.g., all zeros). Inline-skip (skip nums[i] when it equals nums[i-1], and skip l/r past equal neighbors after a hit) never generates duplicates and avoids redundant scans.
